title: Casting Radiographic Appearance and Acceptance Criteria
narrationScript: Last lesson we learned where casting discontinuities are born. Now we learn what they look like on film, and then the part that makes you a Level Two, how a casting is formally accepted or rejected. Start with the physics you already own. Film density follows the metal path the beam crossed. A void means less metal, more radiation reaching the film, and a darker image. Extra dense material means a lighter image. Every casting indication is a variation on that theme, and the shape of the dark or light area is your fingerprint. Gas porosity prints as sharp, dark, rounded spots with smooth outlines, scattered, clustered, or sometimes aligned. A blowhole is just a bigger, smoother version. Shrinkage is the shape shifter, so learn its four costumes. Cavity shrinkage is a dark, irregular patch with ragged, often diffuse edges. Sponge shrinkage looks lacy or feathery, a mottled dark network in a thick section. Filamentary or dendritic shrinkage prints as fine branching dark streaks, like a river system seen from the air, following the way the crystals grew. Micro shrinkage is a faint cloudy darkening you often have to chase with a fresh angle to confirm. A hot tear is a ragged, dark, branched line at a section change or another point of restraint. A crack is finer, straighter, and sharper edged. A cold shut is a smooth continuous dark line, often with rounded ends, sitting where two metal fronts met. Sand inclusions print as irregular dark patches with a grainy texture, and dross does something similar in light alloys. Now the light indications. A chaplet that did not fuse shows a faint dark ring tracing the gap around its rim, while the chaplet body itself blends with the casting. A high density inclusion, a chip of tungsten or other heavy metal, prints as a distinctly light spot. Core shift does not make a spot at all. It makes one wall image consistently darker and the opposite wall lighter, because the core moved and changed the thicknesses. And then there is the impostor, mottling. In coarse grained materials like austenitic stainless and some bronzes, the crystal structure diffracts the beam and paints a false porosity pattern on the film. The test is simple. Re-shoot with the angle changed slightly. Real porosity holds its position and shape. Mottling shifts, changes, or disappears. Calling mottling porosity gets a good casting scrapped. Calling porosity mottling lets a bad casting ship. So how do we judge what we see. Castings are graded by comparison, against published sets of reference radiographs. These are graded plates showing each discontinuity type at increasing severity levels, typically one through five, level one the least severe. The big ones to know. For steel castings up to two inches thick, the standard set most specifications call is A S T M E four forty six. From two to four and a half inches, E one eighty six. From four and a half to twelve inches, E two eighty. Aluminum and magnesium castings use E one fifty five. Aluminum and magnesium die castings use E five oh five. Steel investment castings for aerospace use E one ninety two, and ductile iron has E six eighty nine. Each set organizes discontinuities into lettered categories. Gas porosity. Sand and slag inclusions. Shrinkage, which in the steel sets is split into several distinct types. And then the unforgiving ones, cracks, hot tears, and inserts, meaning unfused chaplets and chills. Here is the point students miss. The graded categories come with severity plates, and the purchase specification states the maximum acceptable level for each category, which can differ by category and by location on the casting. The crack, hot tear, and insert categories generally have no graded plates at all. Under typical specifications they are not acceptable at any level. Found means rejected. Your procedure is comparison, not artistry. Identify the discontinuity type, pick the reference set matching the material and thickness, put the production film beside the plates, and compare the worst area of the casting for that category against the plate for the specified level. Equal to or better than the plate passes. Worse fails. Each discontinuity type is judged against its own category independently. And remember whose number it is. The engineer who wrote the purchase specification chose the severity levels based on service loads. The radiographer's integrity lives in honest comparison, not in deciding what the casting can tolerate.
imagePrompt: A film interpreter in a darkened evaluation room compares a casting radiograph on a high-intensity illuminator against a boxed set of reference radiograph plates spread on the bench beside a densitometer.

sections.heading: From Object to Image
sections.body:
Radiographic appearance follows one rule: film density tracks the metal path the beam crossed. A **void** presents less metal, passes more radiation, and prints **darker**. **Denser-than-parent** material prints **lighter**. Geometry does the rest — the outline, texture, and sharpness of the dark or light area is the fingerprint that identifies the discontinuity.

Two disciplines come before any identification. First, confirm the film itself is valid: density in the required range, the required IQI sensitivity demonstrated, identification and location markers present. Second, rule out **artifacts** — crimp marks, static, pressure marks, processing streaks — by checking whether the suspect image sits in the emulsion (visible by reflected light, often on one side only) rather than in the subject. Only then does the catalog below apply.

sections.caption: Simulated casting radiograph with the classic image of each major discontinuity type labeled.

sections.heading: Reading the Shapes
sections.body:
- **Gas porosity** — sharp, dark, rounded spots with smooth outlines; scattered, clustered, or aligned. **Blowholes** are larger, smoother versions.
- **Cavity shrinkage** — dark irregular patch, ragged and often diffuse-edged, at a thermal center.
- **Sponge shrinkage** — lacy, feathery, mottled dark network in thick sections.
- **Filamentary / dendritic shrinkage** — fine branching dark streaks like a river system, following dendrite growth.
- **Micro-shrinkage** — faint cloudy darkening; often needs a second angle to confirm.
- **Hot tear** — ragged, branched dark line at a section change or restraint point.
- **Crack** — finer, straighter, sharper-edged dark line than a tear.
- **Cold shut** — smooth, continuous dark line, often with rounded ends, at a flow confluence.
- **Sand inclusion** — irregular dark patch with grainy texture; **dross** prints similarly in light alloys.
- **Unfused chaplet or chill** — faint dark ring tracing the unfused gap around the hardware's rim; the body itself blends with parent metal.
- **High-density inclusion** — distinctly **light** spot from heavy contaminating metal.
- **Core shift** — no spot at all: one wall images consistently darker and the opposite wall lighter because thicknesses changed.

sections.title: Mottling: the impostor
sections.body: Coarse-grained alloys (austenitic stainless, some bronzes) **diffract** the beam and paint a false porosity pattern on the film. The test: re-shoot with the beam angle changed slightly. **Real porosity holds its position and shape; mottling shifts, changes, or disappears.** Calling mottling porosity scraps good castings; the reverse ships bad ones.

sections.heading: Reference Radiographs: The Grading System
sections.body:
Castings are evaluated by **comparison** against published sets of graded reference radiographs — plates showing each discontinuity type at increasing severity levels, typically **1 through 5** (1 least severe).

The sets a working interpreter must know:

- **ASTM E446** — steel castings up to 2 in. thick
- **ASTM E186** — heavy-walled steel, 2 to 4.5 in.
- **ASTM E280** — heavy-walled steel, 4.5 to 12 in.
- **ASTM E155** — aluminum and magnesium castings
- **ASTM E505** — aluminum and magnesium **die** castings
- **ASTM E192** — steel investment castings (aerospace practice)
- **ASTM E689** — ductile iron castings

Each set organizes discontinuities into **categories** — gas porosity; sand and slag inclusions; shrinkage (split into several distinct types in the steel sets); and the ungraded categories: **cracks, hot tears, and inserts** (unfused chaplets and chills). The graded categories have severity plates; the ungraded ones generally do not, because under typical specifications **they are not acceptable at any level** — found means rejected.

sections.caption: Common reference-radiograph standards by material and section thickness.
The comparison-grading workflow: production film versus reference plates at the severity level the design specification assigns.

sections.heading: Working With the Plates
sections.body:
Comparison grading has mechanics worth doing properly. Work on a high-intensity illuminator with the production film and the reference plate side by side, edges masked, in a darkened room — the comparison is only fair when both images are viewed under the same conditions. Densities should be reasonably similar; a production film at density 3.5 compared against a much lighter plate exaggerates or hides texture, so note the densities and position the films so the areas being compared get equal light.

Grade **area by area**, not film by film. Mentally divide the casting image and find the worst region for each category present; that region carries the grade for the whole film. Where two discontinuity types overlap in one area — say porosity scattered through sponge shrinkage — each is still compared against **its own** category plate, and each must independently meet its specified level.

Record the outcome the way the spec speaks: category, severity level found, severity level allowed, and the disposition. A casting report that says only 'acceptable' is a weak record; one that says 'category A level 2 found, level 3 permitted' survives an audit.

sections.heading: Applying the Acceptance Criteria
sections.body:
The grading procedure is comparison, not artistry:

- **Identify** each discontinuity type present on the production film.
- **Select** the reference set matching material and thickness, and the **category** matching the discontinuity.
- **Compare the worst area** of the casting for that category against the plate for the severity level the specification names. Equal to or better than the plate passes; worse fails.
- Judge **each category independently** — a casting can pass porosity at level 2 and still fail shrinkage at level 2.

Who sets the level? The **design engineer**, through the purchase order or governing code — pressure-equipment rules, for example, tie casting quality factors to specific severity levels of E446/E186/E280, and may demand tighter levels in critical zones (flange necks, pressure-boundary junctions) than in the body. Severity levels may legitimately differ by category, by location, and by thickness. The radiographer's job is honest identification and honest comparison. Deciding what the casting can tolerate in service is engineering's job — never the film reader's.

figures.title: Simulated casting radiograph: learn the shapes
Grading a casting against reference radiographs

interactive.title: Reject at Any Level
interactive.intro: This casting is being graded to typical purchase-spec severity levels using ASTM reference radiographs. Most of these indications are compared against graded plates — but one belongs to a category with no acceptable level. Click the indication that is rejected outright.

regions.label: Gas porosity cluster
regions.feedback: Gas porosity is a graded category — compare the worst area against the severity plate the specification names. It can pass if at or better than the specified level.

regions.label: Sponge shrinkage
regions.feedback: Shrinkage is a graded category (the steel sets split it into several types). It is judged against the specified severity plate, so some shrinkage can be acceptable.

regions.label: Sand inclusion
regions.feedback: Sand and slag inclusions form a graded category with severity plates — acceptable up to the level the purchase specification assigns.

regions.label: Hot tear
regions.feedback: Correct. Cracks, hot tears, and inserts are ungraded categories — no severity plates exist because typical specifications accept them at no level. A confirmed hot tear is rejected outright.

regions.label: Mottled patch
regions.feedback: Suspect mottling — a diffraction artifact of coarse grain, not a discontinuity. Re-shoot at a slightly different angle: if the pattern shifts or vanishes, it is mottling and is not graded at all.

checkQuestions.question: Fine, branching dark streaks resembling a river system appear in the thick section of a steel casting radiograph. The best identification is:
checkQuestions.explanation: Filamentary or dendritic shrinkage prints as fine branching dark streaks following dendrite growth in last-to-freeze regions; porosity is rounded, a cold shut is a single smooth line, and static shows jagged black lightning-like marks in the emulsion.

checkQuestions.question: A faint dark ring traces a neat circle in a cored region of a sand casting, while the metal inside the ring images like the parent metal. This most likely indicates:
options: An unfused chaplet — the dark ring is the unfused gap around its rim
checkQuestions.explanation: A chaplet that failed to fuse leaves a thin gap around its rim that images as a faint dark ring; the chaplet body, being similar metal, blends with the casting.

checkQuestions.question: A 3-inch-thick steel valve body casting is to be graded by comparison. Which reference radiograph set applies?
checkQuestions.explanation: E186 covers heavy-walled steel castings from 2 to 4.5 in.; E446 stops at 2 in., E280 picks up from 4.5 to 12 in., and E155 is for aluminum and magnesium.

checkQuestions.question: A film of an austenitic stainless casting shows a porosity-like pattern. A second exposure with the beam angle changed slightly shows the pattern shifted and partly disappeared. Conclusion:
options: The casting contains migrating gas porosity and must be rejected
The pattern is mottling — a diffraction artifact of coarse grain, not a discontinuity
checkQuestions.explanation: Real porosity holds position and shape when the angle changes; mottling, caused by diffraction in coarse-grained material, shifts, changes, or vanishes — the standard confirmation test.

checkQuestions.question: Who establishes the maximum acceptable severity level for each graded discontinuity category on a casting radiograph?
options: The radiographer, based on overall film appearance
The film manufacturer, in the film system class
The design engineer, through the purchase specification or governing code
The foundry pourman, based on melt records
checkQuestions.explanation: Severity levels are engineering decisions tied to service loads, stated in the purchase order or code — the radiographer's job is honest identification and comparison against the plates for the specified level.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Radiographic Interpretation and Evaluation Course, IE 3.4–3.6
ASTM reference radiograph standards E446, E186, E280, E155, E505, E192, E689 (general reference)
Pressure-equipment codes tying casting quality factors to reference-radiograph severity levels (general reference)
